page.title: Project work with more context, process, and outcome.
page.description: Featured projects stay at the top, with the rest of the portfolio available below through the expanded archive and filters.
page.searchPlaceholder: Search by title, tool, or tag
page.emptyMessage: No projects match the current search.

projects.title: Solar Farm GIS Project
projects.imageAlt: Solar Farm GIS Project map output
projects.summary: Five-part GIS coursework project identifying candidate solar farm sites on Saddle Lake IR #125 through layered feasibility analysis and cost-per-MWh comparison.
projects.impact: Reduced the full reserve area to four candidate parcels with quantified capacity, annual generation, and infrastructure access cost. Parcel 1 ranked most economical (CostPerMWh 0.196); Parcel 2 offered the largest potential output at 71.9 ha and ~485,000 MWh per year.
projects.role: Spatial analysis, suitability modelling, cartographic output
projects.overview: A five-part coursework project (GEOG 2551/2553) applying layered GIS suitability analysis to Saddle Lake Indian Reserve #125, approximately 130 km northeast of Edmonton. Each assignment built on the last, culminating in a ranked shortlist of four candidate solar farm sites.
projects.challenge: The core challenge was moving from a broad study area to a defendable shortlist of candidate sites while keeping the tradeoffs between legal, economic, and technical constraints visible and comparable.
process: Preprocessed environmental constraint layers (Wildlife Habitat Sensitivity Risk, roads, lakes, streams) and a DEM-derived slope and aspect surface to define exclusion zones and suitable terrain.
Built a combined road and transmission line access cost surface using distance accumulation to estimate infrastructure cost at every cell in the study area.

Intersected legal, economic, and technical feasibility layers to produce a 'Truly Potential Land' layer, then drew four candidate parcels and calculated installed capacity, annual generation, and CostPerMWh for each.
projects.outcome: Four candidate parcels with quantified attributes: Parcel 1 was the most economical site (CostPerMWh 0.196, access cost ~$54,000); Parcel 2 offered the highest output (71.9 ha, ~485,000 MWh/year) at moderate cost.

detailSections.title: What this project was
detailSections.body: A five-part GIS coursework project applying sequential suitability analysis to a real study area - translating environmental, economic, and terrain constraints into a ranked set of candidate solar sites.

detailSections.title: How it was done
detailSections.body: Each assignment contributed a layer to the final result: constraint mapping, terrain derivatives, infrastructure cost surfaces, feasibility intersection, and candidate site creation with CostPerMWh comparison.

detailSections.title: Why it mattered
detailSections.body: The workflow produced a shortlist that could support a real planning discussion - showing how location relative to roads and transmission lines dominated site economics once all constraints were combined.

projects.title: Parks & Pathways Overview
projects.imageAlt: Interactive map showing Calgary parks and pathways layers
projects.summary: Interactive Leaflet map exploring Calgary's green-space network with toggleable parks, trails, and boundary layers.
projects.impact: Turned a course assignment web map into a more useful exploratory interface - going beyond the minimum requirements to add layer controls, filtering, and reset behavior that let viewers compare Calgary's green-space network on their own terms.
projects.role: Web mapping, interface design, GIS communication
projects.overview: Built a responsive web map that helps users visually compare how Calgary parks and pathway corridors relate to one another across the city.
projects.challenge: The goal was to make geographic relationships easier to inspect without overwhelming the user with a heavy GIS-style interface.
process: Loaded parks and trail layers from ArcGIS services and combined them with a local Calgary boundary dataset.
Added layer controls, reset behaviour, and simple filtering so the map could move beyond a static display.
Adjusted the interface to support both desktop and smaller screens while keeping the controls readable.
projects.outcome: Users can toggle layers, filter the visible data, and identify corridors where major park areas appear to connect through the pathway network.

detailSections.title: What this project was
detailSections.body: A front-end web-mapping project designed to communicate spatial relationships clearly through an interactive browser-based map.

detailSections.title: How it was done
detailSections.body: The map uses Leaflet for rendering, Esri-Leaflet for hosted data access, and a lightweight control panel for filtering and view management.

detailSections.title: Why it mattered
detailSections.body: It demonstrates both GIS thinking and practical interface decisions: not just loading spatial data, but shaping how people explore it.

projects.title: Web Games Lab
projects.imageAlt: Illustrated preview card for the Web Games Lab page
projects.summary: Small browser-based games and interactive tools built to make colour reading, logic, and data intuition more hands-on.
projects.impact: Creates a dedicated home for browser-based experiments that would otherwise sit as loose files with no context. Current builds include a two-player Dodgeball game rebuilt from the first game ever programmed (originally in Lua) and an RGB Guesser for building colour-reading intuition.
projects.role: Game design, front-end development, interactive learning
projects.overview: A lightweight page for small browser-based experiments and mini-games that can grow over time without crowding the main portfolio.
projects.challenge: The goal is to keep these projects playful and approachable while still making them feel consistent with the rest of the site.
process: Created a hidden landing page outside the main navigation so the games can live as a side branch of the portfolio rather than competing with the main site structure.
Set up a separate data file and renderer so new games can be added later by extending a list instead of rebuilding the page each time.
Built the first game as a standalone page so each game can have its own rules, styling, and interaction logic without turning one page into a tangle of conditionals.
projects.outcome: The portfolio now has a dedicated Web Games page and a first playable RGB guessing game, with room to expand later.

detailSections.title: Why this section exists
detailSections.body: It gives smaller experimental builds a clear home. That makes it easier to add short interactive pieces that support learning, visual thinking, or technical experimentation.

projects.title: Video Game Patch Analysis
projects.summary: Analyzed Valorant patch 11.08 to quantify whether balance changes shifted agent pick rates and win rates across ranked brackets and professional play.
projects.impact: Identified the sentinel role had dropped below a 20% pick rate - a threshold argued to represent roughly one sentinel per team on average given four roles and five players - and built a structured before/after comparison to support the interpretation rather than relying on community perception.
projects.overview: Treated Valorant patch 11.08 as a structured analysis problem: collect pre- and post-patch agent and role balance data, clean it, compare it across ranked brackets and pro play, and interpret whether the patch actually changed the game's balance in a meaningful way.
projects.challenge: Patch discussion in the Valorant community is typically driven by perception rather than evidence. The goal was to build a before/after comparison that could test whether role viability was genuinely shifting.
process: Collected and cleaned agent pick rate and win rate data across ranked brackets and professional play for pre- and post-patch periods.
Analyzed balance at the role level - duelist, initiator, controller, sentinel - to surface patterns not visible in per-agent numbers alone.
Built visualizations showing where key indicators shifted and framed the sentinel class drop below 20% pick rate as a measurable balance signal, using team composition logic as a reference point.
projects.outcome: Produced a structured analysis showing the sentinel class had dropped below the 20% pick rate threshold post-patch, with supporting evidence across ranked and professional contexts.

detailSections.title: What this project was
detailSections.body: A data analysis project applying structured before/after comparison to Valorant patch 11.08, focused on agent and role balance across competitive play tiers.

detailSections.title: How it was done
detailSections.body: Pick rate and win rate data were collected, cleaned, and analyzed by agent and role across ranked brackets and pro play, with results visualized to surface patterns not obvious from community discussion alone.

detailSections.title: Why it mattered
detailSections.body: It demonstrates practical analysis habits: define the question, structure the data, visualize the signal, and explain the result with a clear and defensible reference point rather than relying on anecdote.

projects.title: Reinforcement Learning Lab
projects.summary: Seven trained RL agents from the Hugging Face Deep RL Course, covering tabular Q-learning, REINFORCE, DQN, PPO, and Unity ML-Agents across classic control, Atari, and continuous environments.
projects.impact: Completed the full Hugging Face Deep RL Course with trained and evaluated agents across five algorithm families, each published to the Hub for reuse and review.
projects.role: Model training, evaluation, experiment tracking, Hugging Face Hub publishing
projects.tools: Python, Stable-Baselines3, Gymnasium, Unity ML-Agents, NumPy, Matplotlib, Hugging Face Hub
projects.overview: A collection of trained reinforcement learning agents published to the Hugging Face Hub, built through the Hugging Face Deep RL Course. The course progressed from tabular Q-learning through policy gradients (REINFORCE), deep Q-networks (DQN), PPO with parallel environments, and Unity ML-Agents.
projects.challenge: Each agent required its own training configuration, environment setup, and evaluation workflow. The goal was to produce trained, evaluated, and packaged agents rather than one-off notebooks.
process: Trained tabular Q-learning agents on FrozenLake-v1 (no-slip) and Taxi-v3 using explicit Q-tables in small discrete environments.
Applied REINFORCE (policy gradient) to CartPole-v1 and PixelCopter, learning to sample and update policies directly from episode returns.
Trained a DQN agent on SpaceInvadersNoFrameskip-v4 and a PPO agent on LunarLander-v3 (302.83 mean reward) using parallel environments and evaluation callbacks.
Trained a Huggy agent using Unity ML-Agents PPO in a custom continuous-action environment.
projects.outcome: Seven trained agents across five algorithm families, each evaluated and published to Hugging Face Hub.

detailSections.title: What this project was
detailSections.body: A structured progression through the Hugging Face Deep RL Course - from tabular methods to deep networks to continuous control - with each agent trained, evaluated, and published.

detailSections.title: How it was done
detailSections.body: Each agent used the appropriate library and training loop for its algorithm family: Q-tables for tabular methods, PyTorch policy networks for REINFORCE, Stable-Baselines3 for PPO and DQN, and Unity ML-Agents for Huggy.

detailSections.title: Why it mattered
detailSections.body: It demonstrates breadth across RL algorithm families and the discipline to follow a structured training and evaluation workflow from start to finish.

projects.title: White Tiger Websites
projects.summary: Responsive multi-page website with reusable layout components and interactive forms.
projects.impact: Demonstrated a complete small-site build with reusable layout patterns, responsive behavior, and form logic rather than isolated page mockups.
projects.role: Front-end development, responsive layout, form behavior
projects.overview: Built a fictional agency website with multiple pages, shared layout components, a pricing/quotes page, and a separate feedback flow.
projects.challenge: The project needed to feel consistent across pages while still supporting page-specific content and interactions.
process: Created a shared structure for navigation, page framing, and footer components across the site.
Used responsive layout techniques so the design adapted across screen sizes.
Added interactive form behavior, including conditionally displayed inputs, to make the pages feel more complete.
projects.outcome: Produced a cohesive site that combined reusable structure with responsive behavior and working user-input patterns.

detailSections.title: What this project was
detailSections.body: A front-end web project focused on consistency, responsiveness, and the practical structure of a small multi-page site.

detailSections.title: How it was done
detailSections.body: The build relied on reusable layout pieces, Bootstrap-assisted structure, and CSS/media-query refinement.

detailSections.title: Why it mattered
detailSections.body: It shows the difference between building a single page and building a site that has to stay coherent across multiple user paths.

projects.title: Calgary Housing Affordability Dashboard
projects.imageAlt: Choropleth map of Calgary communities colour-coded by housing affordability
projects.summary: Interactive choropleth dashboard mapping Calgary housing assessment values by community, with a budget slider that updates affordability shading in real time.
projects.impact: Turned a 40 MB raw assessment dataset into an explorable map where any budget threshold immediately recolours the city's communities - making affordability geography visible at a glance rather than buried in a spreadsheet.
projects.role: Data processing, geospatial visualization, Dash application development
projects.overview: Built an interactive choropleth map joining 2024 Calgary housing assessment data with community boundary GeoJSON. A budget slider lets users set a threshold; communities with mean assessed values within budget appear teal, over-budget communities appear red.
projects.challenge: The raw assessment file contained over 200,000 individual property records. The challenge was aggregating them by community at load time and binding the result to geographic boundaries fast enough for the interactive slider to feel responsive.
process: Loaded and aggregated the 40 MB housing assessment CSV by community name using pandas, computing median assessed values per community.
Joined the aggregated statistics to a Calgary community boundary GeoJSON file to create a single map-ready dataset.
Built a Dash layout with a Plotly choropleth map and a budget range slider; used a callback to re-colour communities each time the slider moves.
projects.outcome: A working interactive dashboard that lets users visually explore which Calgary communities fall within a given budget, with real 2024 assessment data driving the colour mapping.

detailSections.title: What this project was
detailSections.body: A geospatial data application combining property assessment records with community boundary data to make housing affordability geography explorable through a browser-based interface.

detailSections.title: How it was done
detailSections.body: pandas handled the aggregation and join; Dash and Plotly Express rendered the map and wired the slider callback; GeoJSON provided the community boundary geometry.

detailSections.title: Why it mattered
detailSections.body: It demonstrates how data engineering and geospatial visualization can be combined into a tool that answers a practical question - not just a static chart, but something a user can actually explore.

projects.title: Stream Health & Land Cover Classification
projects.imageAlt: Detailed project poster for the Elk Creek stream health land cover classification study
projects.summary: Multi-temporal land cover classification for a stream-health study using machine-learning pixel classification on satellite imagery, with accuracy assessment and documented cartographic outputs.
projects.impact: Produced a validated land cover map comparing 2023 and 2025 conditions across the Elk Creek study area, supported by confusion matrices and a detailed project poster communicating the methodology and findings.
projects.role: Remote sensing, ML classification, accuracy assessment, GIS analysis
projects.tools: ArcGIS Pro, Python, Planet satellite imagery, DEM, Orthoimagery
projects.overview: Classified land cover across two time periods (2023 and 2025) using machine-learning pixel classification in ArcGIS Pro, integrating Planet satellite imagery stacks, a digital elevation model, and orthoimagery for the Elk Creek study area. Accuracy was validated with confusion matrices against independent validation point sets.
projects.challenge: The study area required integrating multiple data sources - multispectral satellite stacks, a DEM, orthoimagery, and precipitation data - and aligning them spatially before classification. Ensuring the classifier generalised across both time periods without retraining from scratch added additional complexity.
process: Assembled and co-registered Planet satellite imagery stacks, DEM derivatives, and orthoimagery for 2023 and 2025 acquisition dates.
Trained a machine-learning pixel classifier in ArcGIS Pro using labelled training samples and evaluated accuracy with confusion matrices and validation point sets.
Compared 2023 and 2025 classified rasters to detect land cover change, and documented the methodology, accuracy results, and findings in a detailed project poster.
projects.outcome: Validated land cover classifications for both time periods with documented accuracy, a change-detection layer highlighting areas of cover transition, and a project poster communicating the full methodology and results.

detailSections.title: What this project was
detailSections.body: An applied remote sensing project using machine-learning classification to map land cover change across two years in support of a stream-health assessment.

detailSections.title: How it was done
detailSections.body: ArcGIS Pro's classification tools processed the satellite stacks and trained the pixel classifier; Python scripts handled precipitation data analysis; accuracy was assessed through confusion matrices against independent validation points.

detailSections.title: Why it mattered
detailSections.body: It demonstrates end-to-end remote sensing workflow - from raw imagery ingestion through classification, validation, and communication - applied to a real environmental monitoring context.

projects.title: Olympic Games Data Analysis
projects.imageAlt: Olympic Games data analysis poster with medal statistics visualizations
projects.summary: Comprehensive analysis of Olympic Games athlete and country performance using a SQLite database, with custom Gold/Silver/Bronze visualizations built in Python.
projects.impact: Produced a full suite of publication-quality figures covering athlete results, country medal aggregations, and podium finish patterns - with a consistent visual identity built around the Olympic medal colour palette.
projects.role: Data analysis, SQL querying, visualization design
projects.tools: Python, SQLite, pandas, NumPy, Matplotlib, Seaborn
projects.overview: Queried an Olympic Games SQLite database to analyse athlete performance, country medal totals, and podium finish distributions. Custom colour palettes matching the Gold, Silver, and Bronze medal hierarchy were applied across all visualizations for visual consistency.
projects.challenge: The database contained overlapping athlete and country relationships across many editions of the Games. Structuring queries to answer multi-level questions - per-athlete, per-country, per-edition - without duplicating records required careful SQL design.
process: Wrote targeted SQL queries across the Olympic database to extract athlete results, country medal counts, and podium distributions.
Used pandas to reshape and aggregate query outputs into analysis-ready frames, then visualized each dimension with Matplotlib and Seaborn.
Defined a custom Gold/Silver/Bronze colour palette and applied it consistently across all figures for a cohesive publication-ready output set.
projects.outcome: A complete set of visualizations covering athlete-level results, country rankings, and podium patterns, with a consistent visual identity aligned to Olympic medal conventions.

detailSections.title: What this project was
detailSections.body: A data analysis project exploring Olympic Games performance data through SQL queries and Python visualization, with a focus on multi-level aggregation and visual consistency.

detailSections.title: How it was done
detailSections.body: SQL handled the querying and filtering; pandas managed the aggregation and reshaping; Matplotlib and Seaborn produced the final figures using a purpose-built medal colour palette.

detailSections.title: Why it mattered
detailSections.body: It shows how SQL and Python visualization work together - and how consistent design choices (the medal palette) can turn a set of separate charts into a coherent analytical story.

projects.title: Multi-format Data Extraction Pipeline
projects.imageAlt: Flowchart showing CSV, JSON, and PDF sources merging into a unified dataset
projects.summary: ETL pipeline that extracts tabular data from CSV, JSON, and PDF sources, cleans and validates each format, then merges them into a single unified dataset.
projects.impact: Automated the extraction of urban growth statistics from three incompatible source formats - including a PDF report with embedded tables - into one clean, analysis-ready CSV with error detection at each stage.
projects.role: Data engineering, ETL design, data cleaning and validation
projects.overview: Built a modular ETL pipeline to unify urban growth data spread across CSV, JSON, and PDF source files. Each format had a dedicated extraction module with its own cleaning and validation logic; a final merger step joined the outputs into one consistent dataset.
projects.challenge: The most difficult source was a PDF report containing embedded tables - standard CSV and JSON readers cannot parse these directly. Identifying and extracting the correct table regions, then normalising the output to match the other sources, required careful handling.
process: Built separate extraction modules for each source format: CSV-to-CSV transformers with error detection, a JSON-to-CSV converter for weather data, and a PDF table extractor using pdfplumber.
Added validation logic to each module to flag missing values, type mismatches, and formatting inconsistencies before the data reached the merger.
Combined the cleaned outputs through a merger step that aligned columns and produced a single unified CSV ready for downstream analysis.
projects.outcome: A working multi-source pipeline that produces a single clean dataset from three incompatible input formats, with validation errors surfaced at each stage rather than silently propagating.

detailSections.title: What this project was
detailSections.body: A data engineering project building a modular ETL pipeline to extract, clean, and unify urban growth data from CSV, JSON, and PDF sources.

detailSections.title: How it was done
detailSections.body: Each source format had its own extraction and validation module; pdfplumber handled table extraction from the PDF report; pandas managed all joining and reshaping.

detailSections.title: Why it mattered
detailSections.body: Real data rarely arrives in one clean format. This project demonstrates the ability to handle messy, heterogeneous inputs and produce reliable output - a core data engineering skill.

projects.title: Calgary Traffic Camera Analysis
projects.imageAlt: Interactive traffic density map of Calgary with hourly slider
projects.summary: End-to-end pipeline that polls Calgary's live traffic camera network, runs YOLOv8 vehicle detection on each feed, spatially joins results to traffic signal infrastructure, and produces an interactive hourly dashboard.
projects.impact: Turned ~300 live municipal camera feeds into a queryable dataset showing where and when Calgary traffic peaks — finding that the NE quadrant carries the highest load city-wide and that all quadrants peak at 4pm.
projects.role: Data engineering, computer vision, geospatial analysis, interactive visualization
projects.tools: Python, YOLOv8, GeoPandas, Plotly, pandas, Requests
projects.overview: A three-stage pipeline built on City of Calgary open data. A collection module polls ~300 live traffic camera feeds every five minutes, downloading frames and running YOLOv8 nano inference to count vehicles by class. A geospatial stage loads camera and traffic signal locations as GeoDataFrames, creates 100-metre buffer zones, and spatially joins signals to their nearest cameras. A visualization stage produces an interactive HTML dashboard: a Mapbox map with an hourly scrubber, a smoothed traffic curve with peak-hour bands highlighted, and per-quadrant trend lines.
projects.challenge: Coordinating reliable real-time data collection across ~300 camera feeds while keeping the downstream spatial and temporal analysis clean enough to surface meaningful patterns rather than just noise.
process: Built a polling loop that fetches live camera frames from the City of Calgary API every five minutes, runs YOLOv8 vehicle detection on each image, and logs timestamped vehicle counts with camera coordinates.
Used GeoPandas to project camera and traffic signal locations into UTM Zone 11N, buffer cameras by 100 metres, and spatial-join signals to identify which infrastructure each camera monitors.

Aggregated observations by camera and hour, applied Gaussian smoothing to the hourly averages, and built three coordinated Plotly figures — an interactive map with an hourly slider, a city-wide traffic curve, and quadrant-level breakdowns — exported as a single HTML dashboard.
projects.outcome: A working automated pipeline producing a self-contained interactive dashboard from live municipal data. Key findings: 4pm is Calgary's busiest traffic hour across all quadrants, and the NE quadrant consistently records the highest vehicle counts.

detailSections.title: What this project was
detailSections.body: An automated data pipeline combining real-time API polling, computer vision vehicle detection, and geospatial analysis to map Calgary traffic patterns from live camera feeds.

detailSections.title: How it was done
detailSections.body: YOLOv8 handled vehicle detection on live camera frames; GeoPandas managed spatial joins between cameras and traffic signal infrastructure; Plotly produced an interactive dashboard with an hourly time slider and smoothed trend lines.

detailSections.title: Why it mattered
detailSections.body: It demonstrates a full data engineering pipeline — from live API ingestion through computer vision, spatial analysis, and interactive output — using real open municipal data rather than a prepared dataset.

projects.title: Urban Expansion in Calgary 2000–2025
projects.imageAlt: ArcGIS Story Map: Urban Expansion in Calgary 2000–2025
projects.summary: An ArcGIS Story Map tracing Calgary's urban footprint growth from 2000 to 2025, examining how and where the city expanded over 25 years.
projects.impact: Makes 25 years of urban growth visible in a spatial narrative, connecting land use change to the broader patterns of how Calgary developed across different periods and directions.
projects.role: Story map authoring, spatial analysis, science communication
projects.overview: An ArcGIS Story Map documenting Calgary's urban expansion from 2000 to 2025. The story map traces the city's growing footprint across 25 years, examining the direction, pace, and pattern of development using spatial data to make the scale of growth legible.
projects.challenge: Presenting 25 years of urban change in a way that communicates both the spatial extent and the temporal pattern of growth without overwhelming the reader with raw data.
process: Compiled and processed spatial data representing Calgary's urban footprint at key intervals between 2000 and 2025.
Structured the narrative to move through time, highlighting major growth periods and the geographic directions of expansion.
Used ArcGIS StoryMaps to combine before/after maps, imagery, and explanatory text into a coherent spatial story.
projects.outcome: An interactive story map that makes Calgary's 25-year urban expansion visible and interpretable through a spatial narrative.

detailSections.title: What this project was
detailSections.body: An ArcGIS Story Map tracing the spatial extent and pattern of Calgary's urban growth from 2000 to 2025 using mapped data and narrative framing.

detailSections.title: How it was done
detailSections.body: Urban footprint data across key time intervals was compiled and visualized, then structured into a temporal narrative using ArcGIS StoryMaps.

detailSections.title: Why it mattered
detailSections.body: Urban expansion is one of the most consequential spatial processes in a growing city — mapping it over time makes the scale and direction of change concrete rather than abstract.

projects.title: Quantifying Local Stream Restoration: Final Results
projects.imageAlt: ArcGIS Story Map: Quantifying Local Stream Restoration Final Results
projects.summary: The final ArcGIS Story Map for the stream restoration quantification project, presenting the completed analysis, real data, and findings after data collection was complete.
projects.impact: Delivers the full results of the stream restoration study in an interactive narrative format, replacing the mock visuals from the intro story map with real outputs and documented findings.
projects.role: Story map authoring, spatial analysis, science communication
projects.overview: The final ArcGIS Story Map completing the stream restoration quantification project. Built after data collection and analysis were finished, this story map presents the real results — replacing the placeholder visuals from the introductory brief with actual maps, charts, and findings from the full study.
projects.challenge: Translating the completed analysis into a clear narrative that connects the original project framing to the actual results, making the findings accessible without losing the methodological rigour behind them.
process: Integrated final analysis outputs — maps, spatial comparisons, and quantified restoration metrics — into the story map narrative.
Structured the presentation to follow from the study context established in the intro brief through to the conclusions.
Used ArcGIS StoryMaps to combine spatial outputs with explanatory text for a mixed technical and general audience.
projects.outcome: A complete final story map presenting the stream restoration study's real data, analysis outputs, and findings in an accessible interactive format.

detailSections.title: What this project was
detailSections.body: The final delivery of the stream restoration quantification project — an ArcGIS Story Map presenting the completed analysis and real results after data collection.

detailSections.title: How it was done
detailSections.body: Final spatial analysis outputs were incorporated into a structured narrative using ArcGIS StoryMaps, building on the framing established in the introductory brief.

detailSections.title: Why it mattered
detailSections.body: The final story map closes the loop from project proposal to results — showing not just what was planned but what was actually found.

projects.title: Canada as an Energy Superpower
projects.imageAlt: ArcGIS Story Map: Canada as an Energy Superpower
projects.summary: An ArcGIS Story Map examining Canada's position as a global energy superpower, exploring its resource base, infrastructure, and the geographic dimensions of energy production and export.
projects.impact: Presents a spatially grounded argument for Canada's energy superpower status, communicating complex resource geography and policy context through an interactive narrative format.
projects.role: Story map authoring, spatial research, science communication
projects.overview: An ArcGIS Story Map exploring Canada's role as a global energy superpower. The story map examines the country's resource base — oil sands, hydropower, natural gas, and renewables — alongside the infrastructure and geographic context that shape how that energy is produced, moved, and exported.
projects.challenge: Communicating the scale and complexity of Canada's energy geography in a way that is accessible and spatially grounded, connecting resource data to broader arguments about energy policy and economic position.
process: Researched Canada's energy resource base and production geography across fuel types and regions.
Structured the narrative to move from resource context through infrastructure to export capacity and policy implications.
Used ArcGIS StoryMaps to integrate maps, data, and explanatory text into a cohesive spatial argument.
projects.outcome: An interactive story map presenting a spatially grounded case for Canada's energy superpower status, readable by both technical and general audiences.

detailSections.title: What this project was
detailSections.body: An ArcGIS Story Map building a geographic and data-driven argument around Canada's position as a global energy superpower.

detailSections.title: How it was done
detailSections.body: Resource and infrastructure data were organized into a narrative arc using ArcGIS StoryMaps, combining spatial context with policy and economic framing.

detailSections.title: Why it mattered
detailSections.body: Energy geography is inherently spatial — story maps are one of the most effective formats for making that spatial argument visible and accessible.

projects.title: Quantifying Local Stream Restoration
projects.imageAlt: ArcGIS Story Map: Quantifying Local Stream Restoration
projects.summary: An introductory ArcGIS Story Map outlining the goals, study context, and proposed approach for quantifying local stream restoration outcomes — produced before data collection and analysis were complete.
projects.impact: Established the project framing, research questions, and methodological intent ahead of the full analysis, using mock visuals to communicate what the final outputs would represent.
projects.role: Story map authoring, project scoping, science communication
projects.overview: An introductory ArcGIS Story Map built at the outset of a stream restoration quantification project. It lays out the study area, research questions, and planned methodology using placeholder and mock visuals — serving as a project brief and communication piece before the actual data collection and analysis took place.
projects.challenge: Communicating the scope and intent of a project clearly before any real data or results are available, using mock visuals to stand in for the outputs that would come later.
process: Defined the study context, research questions, and analysis approach in narrative form.
Used mock visuals to illustrate what the planned outputs — maps, charts, and spatial comparisons — would look like once real data was collected.
Structured the story map to serve as a project brief for collaborators and stakeholders at the project's start.
projects.outcome: A structured introductory story map that communicated the project's intent and scope ahead of the full analysis phase.

detailSections.title: What this project was
detailSections.body: An introductory ArcGIS Story Map produced before data collection began, outlining the study area, research questions, and planned approach for quantifying stream restoration outcomes.

detailSections.title: How it was done
detailSections.body: The story map combined narrative framing with mock visuals to communicate what the analysis would produce, functioning as a project scoping document rather than a results presentation.

detailSections.title: Why it mattered
detailSections.body: Framing a project before the work begins forces clarity about goals and methods — and creates a reference point that the final analysis can be compared against.

projects.title: Procedural Tank Artillery Game
projects.imageAlt: Projectile trajectories over procedural terrain in a tank artillery game
projects.summary: Turn-based artillery game with procedural terrain, projectile physics, and a simple computer opponent.
projects.impact: Combined math, physics, and simulation logic into a playable terminal game that visualized each turn and tracked state over time.
projects.role: Simulation logic, procedural generation, gameplay systems
projects.overview: Implemented a turn-based artillery game in Python where terrain is procedurally generated and each shot is simulated against the landscape.
projects.challenge: The core challenge was combining generation, collision checks, and game-state updates in a way that remained understandable and playable.
process: Generated terrain using randomized sine-based parameters to produce different maps from round to round.
Simulated projectile paths and checked for collisions against the terrain profile.
Tracked tank health and turn outcomes, visualizing the state after each action.
projects.outcome: Created a complete playable loop with procedural terrain, plotted projectiles, and win/loss state tracking.

detailSections.title: What this project was
detailSections.body: A programming and simulation exercise that turned mathematical ideas into a small but complete playable system.

detailSections.title: How it was done
detailSections.body: The project combined procedural terrain creation, per-turn simulation, and terminal-driven game flow.

detailSections.title: Why it mattered
detailSections.body: It shows comfort with implementing logic from scratch and translating technical rules into a working experience.
